summary: Spolsky argues that measuring knowledge worker performance inevitably leads to 'measurement dysfunction,' where workers optimize for the metric rather than actual value. He illustrates this with examples from Amazon and Gateway, where customer service reps gamed call-per-hour metrics by hanging up on customers. He extends the argument to software development, where rewarding code output and bug fixes incentivizes writing buggy code, and to CEO compensation, where stock options incentivize short-term stock inflation over long-term company health. Drawing on Robert Austin's research, he concludes that without 100% supervision, incentive pay tied to measurements is fundamentally broken.
key_points: Amazon reps hung up on customers to boost their calls-per-hour metric, and Gateway reps rushed customers off the phone to meet 13-minute call limits, tanking customer satisfaction
Rewarding programmers for writing lots of code and fixing lots of bugs creates a perverse incentive to write buggy code and then fix it, rather than getting it right the first time
CEO stock option compensation incentivizes inflating stock prices temporarily and selling or quitting at the peak, even at the cost of ruining the company
Measurement dysfunction is inevitable whenever workers cannot be completely supervised, which is almost always the case with knowledge workers
Even if you could accurately measure performance, incentive pay itself is a flawed concept
money_quotes: It seems like any time you try to measure the performance of knowledge workers, things rapidly disintegrate, and you get what Robert D. Austin calls measurement dysfunction.
In the absence of 100% supervision, workers have an incentive to 'work to the measurement,' concerning themselves solely with the measurement and not with the actual value or quality of their work.
The best way to get ahead in an organization like this is to check in lots of buggy code and fix it all, rather than taking the extra time to get it right in the first place.

When you try to fix this problem by penalizing programmers for creating bugs, you create a perverse incentive for them to hide their bugs or not tell the testers about new code they wrote in hopes that fewer bugs will be found.
As a result CEOs do everything they can to inflate the price of the stock, even if it comes at the cost of bankrupting or ruining the company.
Terrific. Now the incentive is to inflate the price of the stock temporarily and then quit. You can't win, again.
key_insight: Measuring knowledge worker performance inevitably produces dysfunction because workers will optimize for whatever metric you choose rather than the actual quality of their work, making incentive pay fundamentally counterproductive.
